title: Basic operators
_authoring: Pedagogical full pass (curriculum audit). Added intro with lessonIntro, per-KC explanations (AND/OR/NOT), and a worked example (A AND B traced four rows). Items reordered: AND → OR → NOT → review. Added l1-review-mix at the end to interleave AND+NOT without composition (no parentheses, no nested sub-expression; NOT applies to a single variable, AND to two atoms, precedence unambiguous) for within-lesson spacing. OR explanation explicitly confronts inclusive-vs-exclusive confusion. All truthTables verified MSB-first. Curriculum-audit fixes (2026-06-01): (1) the truth-table definition (what the grid is, exhaustive rows, 4 rows for 2 inputs) is folded INTO the opening AND explanation, which carries an illustrative AND truth table — so the lesson still opens on AND (not on an abstract 'Truth tables' card) but the learner sees and understands a real grid before the first table-filling challenge; (2) walk-through steps rewritten to explain reasoning at each row rather than asserting results; (3) circuit/pseudocode step demoted to a brief no-action preview, and workedExample.visibleReps narrowed to truth_table only; (4) l1-and varied from A AND B to B AND A (same truth table, forces retrieval rather than pattern copy); (5) l1-review-mix changed from compound (A OR B) AND NOT A to non-compound A AND NOT B (interleaves AND+NOT without nested composition).

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 1 — Basic Operators: AND, OR, NOT
lessonIntro.body: Boolean logic is the algebra of true and false. Every circuit, every conditional in code, every search filter ultimately reduces to three primitive operations: AND, OR, and NOT. In this lesson you will build and read truth tables for all three — the skill that underlies every lesson that follows.

explanations.body: AND is written A & B. It produces true ONLY when BOTH inputs are true — if either input is false, the output is false. The grid below is its truth table: every possible combination of the inputs (one row each) and the output for each, so it's the complete map of what AND does. You'll read and build these all lesson; with 2 inputs there are always 4 rows.

OR is written A || B. It produces true when AT LEAST ONE input is true — including when both are true. This is the inclusive OR of logic, and it differs from everyday English 'or', which usually means 'one or the other but not both'. In code-like logic, 1 || 1 = 1. The only case where OR is false is when every input is false.

NOT is written !A. It flips the value: !1 = 0 and !0 = 1. It is a unary operator (takes exactly one input). In circuit diagrams it is drawn as a bubble or inverter. In pseudocode it is written NOT A or !A.

steps.label: List all input combinations
steps.detail: A and B are each either 0 or 1, so there are 2 × 2 = 4 possible combinations — and you must list every single one; skip any row and the table is incomplete. The canonical order is MSB-first (as defined above): A is the most significant bit, so it changes most slowly. The four rows in order are: (A=0,B=0), (A=0,B=1), (A=1,B=0), (A=1,B=1).

steps.label: Apply AND to each row
steps.detail: AND rule: output is 1 only when BOTH inputs are 1; otherwise 0. Row 1: A=0, B=0 — not both 1 (neither is 1) → 0. Row 2: A=0, B=1 — not both 1 (A is 0) → 0. Row 3: A=1, B=0 — not both 1 (B is 0) → 0. Row 4: A=1, B=1 — both are 1 → 1.

steps.label: Read the output column
steps.detail: Reading the output column top to bottom gives [0, 0, 0, 1] — one entry per row, in the same canonical order. Notice the pattern: only one 1, and it sits in the very last row where both inputs are 1. This single-1-at-the-bottom shape is the signature of AND. It tells you AND is demanding: every input must be true, and any single 0 anywhere kills the output.

steps.label: Coming up: circuit and pseudocode forms
steps.detail: You will also meet AND as a circuit symbol (a D-shaped logic gate) and in code ('if A AND B then ...'). The same rule — both must be true — drives all three forms. No action needed now; just note that the truth table you just built is the same truth the gate enforces and the same condition the code tests. You will explore those representations in later steps.
